Now, we want number two, which is past the one you're supposed to actually ask for a customer referral. Now, the truth is, and this is not completely cut and drive, by the way, but you have to be smart here and be a little bit intuitive as well, but one of the biggest mistakes that salesmen make is that they typically will ask for a referral very, very early in the game, in fact, right after they close the sale. As opposed to waiting a couple of days and then asking for the referral after you're checking in as part of your normal post-sale first touch point. And, of course, you never know for sure what you're going to find at that point, right? They might still be skeptical about things. Maybe they have some questions about something or they might even have a problem. But either way, what you need to remember here, and this is really powerful, is that it's a whole new relationship now because you're going to approach them from the position of being a trusted friend, not a salesperson, not calling them back to sell them so that you can see how things are and to make sure they're happy. You get that? See, this is the point where you can really, really get that goodwill established. If they have a question, then you answer it. And if they have a problem, then you do your best to solve it right on the spot yourself, and then you ask for the referral. And if you couldn't solve it yourself, then you bring them to someone who can and you stay involved. You don't just simply pass the buck and say, yeah, I'll have someone from customer service call you back. Have a great day. Oh, by the way, anyone you can refer me to, if you do that, the only thing you'll probably get is a really quick click in your ear. So again, in that situation, the best thing to do is direct them to the person who can actually solve their problem while staying involved as much as possible. And then once things are back on track, then you ask for the referral. Makes sense? It's very powerful. And if you do that, the amount of goodwill you establish is staggering. And by the way, there are, of course, exceptions to the rule about waiting for this first touch point. Remember I said you need to be intuitive and a bit flexible. If you happen to be in super tight rapport with someone right after you close the sale and the opportunity presents itself automatically, meaning it's not initiated by you, then go for it. You know, ask their referral right on the spot. And if you get one or two, excellent. But if you don't, don't push. You'll have another chance in three or four days when you call them back as part of that normal post sale first touch point and you ask them again then and believe your numbers will go up dramatically. Okay. Thanks. Bye. Bye. Bye. Bye. Bye. Bye. Bye.
